sentences.text: I was sent to the infirmary, supposedly to heal, by Dr.
Josef Mengele.
Simply because I had a rash from-- from the heat, and the humidity, and the filth, the dust, of Auschwitz.
I obviously reacted to it.
But in order to make sure that it's not something contagious, he isolated me by sending me to an infirmary.
It-- It was just a little shack that is not a hospital, where there was no medicine.
But there was a Hungarian inmate doctor.
We called her Dr.
Gizzy.
A little bitty woman-- she came up to my shoulders, and she spoke Hungarian to me.
And I remember we slept the same as in barracks, in three tiers-- three-tier bunks.
And after the-- I forget-- the second night, I think it was-- the second or third night-- I think the second night.
Anyway, after the second night, Dr.
Gizzy came to me and said, 'Go back to your mother.' I said, 'You're releasing me.' 'Yes, because tonight there will be a [?
Razia.' ?] 'What's a [?
Razia?' ?] 'Well, tonight they're going to come and pick up everybody who is unable to go back to the barrack.' I said, 'Thank you, Dr.
Gizzy.' In the meantime, nothing was done here on it.
I remember just sitting on some warm-- some warm heater.
And that's all.
But it was miserable there.
Everybody was crying and moaning.
They were hurting.
I wasn't hurting.
I was perfectly healthy.
I was afraid I'll catch something among all these people.
But I-- it was dark, and I was afraid I'll get shot if I go back to the barrack by myself.
So I hesitated.
And so Dr.
Gizzy called somebody and said, 'Take her to such and such place.
And from there, you come back, and she'll make her way back.' And that's what I did, and I went back to the barrack and was reunited with my mother again.
I'd like to share with you another daily-- almost daily event, in Auschwitz-Birkenau.
I think you now understand how we-- each morning, we had to walk through different electric wire fences-- the gates through them-- to-- to go to work.
At a certain point, just before we entered the electric wire fence gate into the forested area, there would be, very often, important visitors. 'Important' visitors.
Heinrich Himmler, the head of the SS, the head of the Gestapo and the secret police, head of all the concentration camps, came one day with Hermann Goring, who was the head of the German Air Force.
And next to Hitler, he was the most powerful man in Germany.
As we were counted off right there, before entering into the forested area where the death factory really is, there was Goring, and there was Himmler.
I couldn't believe my eyes.
I saw pictures of them in newspapers, so I recognized Himmler particularly, with his little rim-wire glasses.
And I was in the outer file, nearest him.
And we were marching to work.
And as I reached close to him, he gave me a kick in the behind with his boot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
It didn't hurt.
It's just that I sometimes wonder, didn't he have anything better to do?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Another event I'd like to talk about.
This very same place, just before we entered the forested area-- very often, we would have a little ensemble, a little small orchestra, playing music.
And as the inmates from the trains would-- or the newly arrived inmates-- would go through that fence, that gate, they went to music very often.
And we-- in the morning, as we marched to work, we listened to beautiful music in the summer of 1944.
That's the only summer that I spent in Auschwitz-- that summer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll never forget the cellist.
A very thin, fair young woman, hair shaven, as all of us had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She just fell off her little chair.
It was a folding chair.
And her instrument was there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gathering dust.
And this hot sun is coming down.
And there, nobody-- nobody gave a damn.
There she was, fainted.
She probably was thirsty, hungry, exhausted.
God knows how many hours she must have practiced on top of it.
And-- And the SS would come and listen to beautiful music.
I know from reading, much later, that they gave concerts to the SS, but I never heard such a concert at Auschwitz-Birkenau.
Never.
Another time, I would see another member of the small ensemble, again, the same way.
Instrument on the ground, and-- either a violinist or another stringed instrumentalist.
So it was really-- they were treated the same way as we were.
The only difference was that they wore blue skirts and white blouses.
That was their uniform, a two-piece ensemble.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sometimes think of this as being very strange-- to-- to have fine music of Beethoven, Brahms-- you name them-- play in Auschwitz-Birkenau, in a death factory.
Yes.
Yes, there-- there was.
Although, I suppose the men had a stronger showing of that, because of the 10 men that's needed in order to create a service, a minyan. 10 men is called a minyan.
In the women-- in the Orthodox movement, women are not counted in-- in a minyan.
But I belong to a-- the Conservative movement, which is sort of like the Democratic party, in the middle of the road.
And-- And in our synagogue, a woman is counted as one of the 10.
But in Auschwitz-Birkenau, the women would find-- we would find them davening, praying, in the corner of the barrack, very, very intently, and sometimes with tears.
And-- And others prayed in their-- in their beds, in their bunk beds.
Others gave up on religion.
And there were still others who-- who cared.
Who cared, and who were confused, such as myself.
I come from an Orthodox family.
We had a strictly kosher household of the first 14 years of my life, until Auschwitz.
And my grandpa was a Talmud Chacham knowledgeable in the Talmud, and my granduncle as well.
And was-- taught the whole area-- the teachers in the whole area-- in his-- his time.
And I found it beautiful, and peaceful, and soothing, and strengthening.
When I went to Auschwitz, my mother remained strong throughout the whole ordeal.
I had-- there were times when I really questioned whether there was a God.
And I wondered, if there was a God, how could he see all this terrible disaster to happen?
This calamity to come down on us?
[SOBS] And yet, this-- this feeling of neither here nor there would make me feel weaker.
It would-- I felt-- this was particularly true later, when I no longer was with my mother and sister.
When I was on my own.
When I really needed somebody to lean on.
And I felt God was my best friend.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could see things happen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes somebody would die without any previous warning, and not from being emaciated.
Sometimes somebody would be alive, very emaciated.
And somehow-- and would be very religious-- I'm not saying only, but it seems to have added to their strength.
If they really believed, it added to their strength.
However, I was confused.
Not while I was with Mother.
But after, I no longer no longer had her magnetism to give me strength, but I'll go into that later.
Here in Auschwitz, we were still together.
And one day, we had a selection, again-- we went through-- by this time, I went through a number of Dr.
Mengele's dreaded selections, you know, where he would-- we would have to undress, and he would decide who should live, and who should die.
And this time, I, too, was sent to the other side, along with 30 other women and girls.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were placed in a barrack.
And the last time I saw-- saw my mother was at this scene.
Naked, in front of Dr.
Josef Mengele.
And she broke down and cried, because I was separated from her.
But this time, I wasn't sent to be recovered, but I was sent to the gas chamber.
I was selected for extermination.
To die.
And apparently, Dr.
Mengele felt that I was no longer capable of keeping up with the others, and I was too emaciated to carry on.
I didn't think so.
But it wasn't up to me, either.
And I knew what this meant.
Late at night, a truck came to take us all away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One SS led us to the truck, and another, this Hungarian guard, came to close the canvas in the back.
And just as he was about to finish closing it, he sort of looked in, and there I was.
I was among the last ones to enter the truck, so I was pretty much in-- in front now.
He said to me in Hungarian, [? 'Te ?] is?' Meaning, 'You, too?' And I just nodded.
This was the Hungarian guard who used to guard us at our Canada work detail, and into whose pocket my sister put a few things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the very guard who was there, closing the canvas in the back.
After he saw me there, he told all of us, 'You all know that we are going to the gas chamber.
Those of you who would want to could jump off.
But if you are found, you must not give me away, for I may be able to save other lives yet.
But if you do give me away, both you and I will be caught, killed.' And with that, he closed the canvas drapes, and he went up front, and he slowly drove off.
Now, the gas chambers were a good distance away.
It took us about a solid hour to march there each morning and back to our barracks at night.
I had ample time to-- to know what the immediate area looks like.
I knew that I am going to jump off that truck.
I thought to myself, if I stayed on this truck, I will die.
If I jumped off, I might be able to save myself and perhaps see my mother again, and my sister, too.
So when that truck approached that familiar wooded area, where there was a deep ditch near the road, I jumped off the slow moving truck, and I leaped down the embankment.
And at the bottom, there was a culvert.
I couldn't see that from the roadside.
It was too far down to be seen.
But I could tell that there was a deep ditch, as we marched to work each day and marched back at night.
And I crawled into this culvert.
I stayed there that night, the following day, and until the following night-- in the middle of the night some time-- without any food or clothing.
In fact, soft ice formed along the sides of the water that ran through the culvert.
And there I was.
After about 15 minutes or so, I heard the sirens go on, which meant-- to alert the guards, that the guards had to be alerted.
And in another 15 minutes or so-- I don't know exactly, of course-- I heard the voices of German men.
And obviously the guards, they were looking for me.
But after a while, the commotion had died down.
I stayed in this culvert for about 24 hours, roughly, without any food or clothing.
Then I decided that if I stayed in that culvert, I am going to die, simply from the cold-- from the elements, and from starvation.
Now I'd like to go back a few steps.
While we were still on the truck, I said, 'Who will come with me?' And nobody responded.
Everyone was completely emaciated and robbed of all energy and willpower-- in fact, probably even the very last shred of hope.
But, you see, I had my loving mother and sister to live for, that hardly anybody else had.
And so I decided to make the jump.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Then, in the middle of that night, I just made my way up to the roadside from where I jumped originally.
Now, it was war time, and there was a complete blackout observed.
But a little light managed to seep through here and there from the barracks.
Now, I was limited by the electric wire fences.
And I didn't know what was in this barrack where the little-- little light shone through.
It was just a tiny little light, much like a little star in the distance.
So I decided to just follow this little light.
It could have been a man's camp.
It could have been the SS headquarters-- I wouldn't have known.
But instead, I thank God provided me with a women's barrack.
Now, these women were transferred there the previous day, and they were going to be shipped out to another camp.
So they were waiting for the train.
Now, I entered this barrack.
And there was no sentry in front.
I just was able to enter it, and I could see the-- the foot of the first bunk bed, and I headed toward that.
And I quickly climbed up to the top, and suddenly, somebody started to scream.
And I could see that she will put me in danger, so I quickly cupped my hand on my mouth to indicate to her not to give me away.
And I explained to her my predicament.
She took off her men's overcoat and put it around me.
She slept in it.
And that night, she took an overcoat off of a dead body.
Which was not an unusual way for the inmates to improve their wardrobe, which consisted of what we wore.
So the following morning, I melted into this group as we lined up for [?
Zahlappell, ?] which is concentration camp lingo for head count.
And as we were counted off, I was counted off with about 600, 700, or 800 women, roughly, taken to the railroad platform, and the train took us away from Auschwitz.
Well, I was wondering about that myself.
You see, there was much chaos by this time, so many people dying, that I think perhaps that some of their fine bookkeeping system broke down.
Anyway, there was only this one barrack.
It wasn't like the other camps, where there were so many of us.
I-- Maybe somebody died.
I don't know.
Or maybe somebody stayed in the barrack.
I have no idea.
But I had no problem, and that was it.
Oh, yes.
I think that became important for me, particularly in the-- in the remaining six camps that I went through, where I no longer was with Mother.
You see, my mother and my sister thought that I was dead probably about an hour and a half after I was picked out.
Well, [SIGHS] that's about how long the whole process took.
And so I was really very much afraid that she-- her life will be in jeopardy because I-- because I was selected for extermination.
And that in the process, Annuska, my sister-- this would drag her down, and that I would lose both my mother and my sister.
And this was my main concern right after this-- this episode that happened to me.
While I was in the camp, I think all of us found it easier to socialize-- the very limited way that we socialized-- with people who spoke our languages.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of us spoke several languages.
And, you see, the-- the hunger and the long work days put us in such physical condition that we had no incentive to make new friends.
Secondly, it really was discouraged by the [? blockalteste. ?] That means the eldest of the block.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She wasn't really the eldest, but she was picked by the SS to do this job-- to keep an eye on us.
So we usually tried to converse with those of us who were family members, or from our-- those who came from our town, or those who spoke our languages.
I think that's-- that had to be simple.
This became a little different when I moved onto another camp, and another camp, and so on, until I went through seven camps.
When I no longer had my mother, and sister, and my first cousins, a few first cousins who were with me in Auschwitz.
Then, I, being the sociable person that I am, I had the need for human warmth, and love, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always made friends.
[SOBS] This girl who gave me her overcoat came from not-- less than 20 miles from our hometown.
And she even knew about our family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She thought we were even related.
It was really incredible.
She was in several camps with me, and then I-- sort of, my memory-- I can't remember what happened to us.
But eventually, in the end, we were liberated together.
And I have pictures of-- a picture of her [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Sweden.
But to get back to Auschwitz, then we-- we travelled, I think, two or three days, and we landed in-- in Bergen-Belsen.
That was my second camp.
And I was really glad to be alive, but I-- I missed my mother and my sister sorely after Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, through research, I found that I must have been on the-- on the last transport to-- from Auschwitz to Bergen-Belsen in the year of 1944.
The last transport left Auschwitz on the 30th and arrived in Bergen-Belsen on the 1st.
So I guess it was two days.
Because in my research, I found that the-- the train that left there prior to that, to Bergen-Belsen from Auschwitz, was back in previous months, and I knew that that could not have been for me.
So through process of elimination, I was able to zero in on that-- on that train.
And if that is correct, I arrived in Bergen-Belsen on January 1st, 1945.
And there in Bergen-Belsen, we were placed in huge canvas barracks.
They were not wooden barracks like in Auschwitz, but made of canvas-- temporary.
Because the camp was so full, and so many inmates were transferred there, that they had to build additional shelter.
I remember there were no bunk beds there.
We arrived in-- in a terrible storm, and we were soaking wet when we arrived in Bergen-Belsen.
I mean, we had to walk from the train to our bunk beds, to the camp, and I couldn't believe my eyes when I saw all that mud flowing in under the canvas from the hillside.
In 1991, I think it was, when I returned to Bergen-Belsen, I couldn't believe how flat the place was.
But I knew approximately where I slept, where this tent barrack must have been, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
It was close to the crematorium, which also burned day and night.
Would-- would you mind if I go back to Auschwitz just for a moment?
I failed to say that, through the terrible smell, I eventually lost my sense of smell in Auschwitz-Birkenau.
And I'd like to say more about that in a second.
